schools.summary: The exclusion of the maimed from the congregation: 'he that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off, shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD' — the man maimed in this way, often by the heathen for idolatrous service, was excluded from the assembly (the he that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off, the maimed, the shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD, the exclusion from the assembly, the figure, the exclusion of the maimed, often connected with heathen idolatry, from the holy assembly, the truth that the congregation of the LORD was to be kept pure, certain disqualifications barring entrance, the figure of the holiness required for access to God, the ceremonial bar a shadow of the purity God requires, the exclusion that points to the perfect access opened in Christ, where the eunuch is welcomed, 'let not the eunuch say, Behold, I am a dry tree... I will give them an everlasting name,' Isaiah 56:3-5, the ceremonial separation under the law fulfilled in the gospel's welcome, Acts 8:27-39); the man maimed, often by heathen idolatry (the disqualification) — the maimed (excluded); 'shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD' (the exclusion from the assembly, the purity of the congregation, Isaiah 56:3-5, the figure, the holiness required for access, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the exclusion, the bastard and the Ammonite to follow, vv.2-3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The exclusion of the bastard to the tenth generation: 'a bastard shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD; even to his tenth generation shall he not enter into the congregation of the LORD' — the bastard, the child of incest or forbidden union, was barred from the assembly to the tenth generation (the a bastard shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD, the exclusion of the bastard, the even to his tenth generation shall he not enter into the congregation of the LORD, to the tenth generation, the figure, the exclusion of the bastard, a mark against unlawful unions, the purity of the assembly, the truth that the sin of unlawful union was visited in the exclusion of the offspring, a deterrent and a mark of God's holiness, the figure of the ceremonial separation that guarded the assembly's purity, fulfilled in the gospel where none who come to Christ are cast out, the tenth generation a figure of a long but not endless exclusion, the shadow giving way to grace, the holiness of the congregation guarded by the law, vv.1); the bastard barred from the assembly (the purity) — the exclusion (the bastard); 'even to his tenth generation shall he not enter' (the long exclusion to the tenth generation, the deterrent against unlawful unions, the figure, the purity of the assembly guarded, the providence, the exclusion, the Ammonite and Moabite to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The exclusion of the Ammonite and Moabite for ever: 'an Ammonite or Moabite shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD; even to their tenth generation shall they not enter into the congregation of the LORD for ever' — the Ammonite and Moabite were barred from the assembly, for their hostility to Israel (the an Ammonite or Moabite shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD, the exclusion of these nations, the even to their tenth generation shall they not enter... for ever, the lasting exclusion, the figure, the exclusion of Ammon and Moab, for their enmity to God's people, the truth that the nations who hired Balaam and refused bread and water were barred, the figure of the judgment on the enemies of God's people, the exclusion from the assembly, the lasting bar, yet not without exception, as Ruth the Moabitess was received, the enmity that excluded these nations, vv.4); Ammon and Moab barred from the assembly (the enmity) — the exclusion (the nations); 'even to their tenth generation shall they not enter... for ever' (the lasting exclusion of these nations, the judgment on enmity, the figure, the exclusion for hostility to God's people, the providence, the exclusion, the reason, their refusal and hiring of Balaam to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The reason for the exclusion, their inhospitality and hiring of Balaam: 'because they met you not with bread and with water in the way, when ye came forth out of Egypt; and because they hired against thee Balaam the son of Beor of Pethor of Mesopotamia, to curse thee' — Ammon and Moab were excluded for refusing kindness and hiring Balaam to curse Israel (the because they met you not with bread and with water in the way, the inhospitality, the and because they hired against thee Balaam... to curse thee, the hiring of Balaam, the figure, the double crime, inhospitality and the hire of Balaam, the ground of exclusion, the truth that their unkindness and malice against God's people were not forgotten, the figure of the enmity that God requites, the curse sought against his people, the inhospitality that refused even bread and water, the malice that hired a curse, the crimes that excluded these nations, Numbers 22:5-6); the refusal of bread and water (the unkindness) — the inhospitality (no bread); 'they hired against thee Balaam... to curse thee' (the hiring of Balaam to curse Israel, the malice, Numbers 22:5-6, the figure, the enmity that God requites, the providence, the reason, God turning the curse to blessing to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That God turned Balaam's curse into a blessing because he loved Israel: 'nevertheless the LORD thy God would not hearken unto Balaam; but the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee' — the curse turned — 'the LORD thy God would not hearken unto Balaam; but the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee': God would not let Balaam curse Israel, but turned the intended curse into blessing, for his love (the witnesses noting the significance — the sovereign love of God that overrules the malice of the enemy, Balaam hired to curse, but God made him bless, the curse turned to blessing by God's hand, the lesson — that God's love guards his people, no curse can prevail against those he loves, the enemy's curse made void, turned to its opposite by God, the ground was not Israel's worth but God's love, the truth that God overrules the designs of the wicked for the good of his people, 'there is no enchantment against Jacob, neither is there any divination against Israel,' Numbers 23:23, the figure — the sovereign love and providence of God that makes the curse a blessing, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, the truth that all things, even the enemy's malice, work together for good to them that love God, 'all things work together for good to them that love God,' Romans 8:28, the figure of the believer guarded by God's love, no weapon formed against him prospering, 'no weapon that is formed against thee shall prosper,' Isaiah 54:17, the truth that God's electing love is the security of his people, the curse of the enemy turned to blessing, the warning to the enemies of God's people, that their curses recoil and become blessings to the elect, the figure of God's sovereign love that turns the curse into a blessing); God would not let Balaam curse Israel, the enemy's design overruled (the malice made void, Numbers 23:23) — the refusal (God not hearkening); 'but the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee' (the curse turned to blessing by God's sovereign love, the ground not Israel's worth but his love, the electing love, Romans 8:28; 8:31; Isaiah 54:17) — the figure (the sovereign love that guards his people, the providence, the love, the charge not to seek Ammon and Moab's peace to follow, vv.6) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why was Balaam's curse turned into a blessing?

sides.position: Because of God's sovereign love for his people - God overruling the malice of the enemy, the hired curse made void and turned to its opposite by his hand, the ground his love and not Israel's worth (Numbers 23:23), a figure of the love that makes all things, even the enemy's malice, work for good to them that love God (Romans 8:28; 8:31) and lets no weapon against the elect prosper (Isaiah 54:17)

schools.summary: The charge not to seek the peace of Ammon and Moab: 'thou shalt not seek their peace nor their prosperity all thy days for ever' — Israel was not to seek alliance or welfare with Ammon and Moab (the thou shalt not seek their peace, no alliance, the nor their prosperity all thy days for ever, no seeking their welfare, the figure, the charge to keep separate from these hostile nations, no league with them, the truth that the enmity of these nations was to be answered by no alliance, the figure of the separation from the enemies of God, no peace with the malicious, the bar against league with those who cursed God's people, the lasting separation from Ammon and Moab, vv.3-5); the charge to seek no peace with these nations (the separation) — the charge (no peace); 'nor their prosperity all thy days for ever' (no seeking their welfare, the lasting separation, the figure, the separation from the enemies of God, the providence, the charge, the Edomite and Egyptian not to be abhorred to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the Edomite and Egyptian were not to be abhorred: 'thou shalt not abhor an Edomite; for he is thy brother: thou shalt not abhor an Egyptian; because thou wast a stranger in his land' — the Edomite, as a brother, and the Egyptian, as a former host, were not to be abhorred (the thou shalt not abhor an Edomite; for he is thy brother, the Edomite a brother, the thou shalt not abhor an Egyptian; because thou wast a stranger in his land, the Egyptian a former host, the figure, the kindness to be shown the Edomite and Egyptian, the remembrance of kinship and hospitality, the truth that even old grievances were to be tempered by the remembrance of brotherhood and former shelter, the figure of the generosity that remembers kindness received, the stranger's land not forgotten, the contrast with Ammon and Moab, the kindlier dealing with Edom and Egypt, the gratitude for past hospitality, the brotherhood of Edom, vv.8); the Edomite not to be abhorred, being a brother (the kinship) — the brotherhood (the Edomite); 'thou shalt not abhor an Egyptian; because thou wast a stranger in his land' (the Egyptian a former host, the kindness remembered, the gratitude for shelter, the figure, the generosity that remembers kindness, the providence, the kindness, their children entering in the third generation to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge to keep from every wicked thing when the host goes out: 'when the host goeth forth against thine enemies, then keep thee from every wicked thing' — in time of war the camp was to be kept free of all wickedness (the when the host goeth forth against thine enemies, the host in the field, the then keep thee from every wicked thing, the charge to purity, the figure, the camp of war to be kept holy, free of every wickedness, the truth that the army of God was to be pure, sin to be put away, the figure of the holiness required where God is present, the camp kept clean, the spiritual warfare that requires purity, the soldier of God to keep from evil, the charge that grounds the laws of the camp's purity, vv.10-14); the host going out to war (the campaign) — the war (the host out); 'keep thee from every wicked thing' (the charge to keep the camp pure, the holiness in war, the figure, the army of God kept clean, the providence, the charge, the laws of cleanness in the camp to follow, vv.10-14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The man unclean by night to go outside the camp: 'if there be among you any man, that is not clean by reason of uncleanness that chanceth him by night, then shall he go abroad out of the camp, he shall not come within the camp' — the man who became unclean was to go outside the camp until cleansed (the if there be among you any man, that is not clean by reason of uncleanness that chanceth him by night, the uncleanness, the then shall he go abroad out of the camp, he shall not come within the camp, out of the camp, the figure, the unclean to be put outside the camp, the holiness of the camp guarded, the truth that even ceremonial uncleanness was not to remain in the holy camp, the figure of the separation of the unclean, the camp kept pure for God's presence, the bodily uncleanness a shadow of the moral purity God requires, the law that guards the camp's holiness, vv.11); the man become unclean (the ceremonial defilement) — the uncleanness (by night); 'he shall go abroad out of the camp, he shall not come within the camp' (the unclean put outside the camp, the holiness guarded, the figure, the camp kept pure, the providence, the law, the washing and return at evening to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The unclean washing and returning at evening: 'but it shall be, when evening cometh on, he shall wash himself with water: and when the sun is down, he shall come into the camp again' — the unclean man was to wash and return to the camp at evening (the but it shall be, when evening cometh on, he shall wash himself with water, the washing, the and when the sun is down, he shall come into the camp again, the return at evening, the figure, the washing that cleansed and restored to the camp, the purity recovered, the truth that the unclean was restored by washing, the camp's holiness maintained, the figure of the cleansing that restores to fellowship, the washing of water, the bodily washing a shadow of the cleansing that restores the soul, 'wash you, make you clean,' Isaiah 1:16, the restoration to the camp after cleansing, vv.10); the washing of the unclean (the cleansing, Isaiah 1:16) — the washing (with water); 'when the sun is down, he shall come into the camp again' (the return to the camp after cleansing, the restoration, the figure, the cleansing that restores to fellowship, the providence, the return, the law of cleanliness in the camp to follow, vv.12-14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The place appointed outside the camp for cleanliness: 'thou shalt have a place also without the camp, whither thou shalt go forth abroad' — a place was to be appointed outside the camp for the relief of nature (the thou shalt have a place also without the camp, the appointed place, the whither thou shalt go forth abroad, for the relief of nature, the figure, the appointed place outside the camp, the cleanliness of the camp guarded, the truth that even in such matters the camp was to be kept clean, the figure of the decency and order that God requires, the camp kept pure, the care for cleanliness as part of the camp's holiness, the place appointed that the camp not be defiled, vv.13); the appointed place outside the camp (the cleanliness) — the place (without the camp); 'whither thou shalt go forth abroad' (the place for the relief of nature, the cleanliness guarded, the figure, the decency God requires, the providence, the place, the covering of waste to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The covering of waste, that the camp be kept clean: 'and thou shalt have a paddle upon thy weapon; and it shall be, when thou wilt ease thyself abroad, thou shalt dig therewith, and shalt turn back and cover that which cometh from thee' — each soldier was to carry a paddle to dig and cover his waste (the and thou shalt have a paddle upon thy weapon, the paddle carried, the and it shall be, when thou wilt ease thyself abroad, thou shalt dig therewith, the digging, the and shalt turn back and cover that which cometh from thee, the covering, the figure, the care for cleanliness, the waste to be covered, the camp kept pure, the truth that even the lowliest matters were ordered for the camp's holiness, the figure of the decency and order that befit God's presence, nothing unclean left exposed, the cleanliness that honours God's presence in the camp, the order required where God walks, vv.14); the paddle carried to dig (the cleanliness) — the digging (with the paddle); 'shalt turn back and cover that which cometh from thee' (the waste covered, the camp kept clean, the decency, the figure, the order that befits God's presence, the providence, the covering, the reason, God walking in the camp to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That God walks in the midst of the camp, so it must be holy: 'for the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp, to deliver thee, and to give up thine enemies before thee; therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee' — the presence — 'for the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp, to deliver thee... therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee': the camp was to be holy because God himself walked in it (the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness required by the presence of God among his people, God present in the camp to deliver and give victory, his presence demanding purity, the lesson — that the presence of God requires holiness, nothing unclean to be tolerated where he dwells, the camp kept pure lest God see uncleanness and turn away, the reason of all the laws of the camp, God's presence, the truth that God dwells among his people and requires them to be holy, 'be ye holy; for I am holy,' 1 Peter 1:16; Leviticus 11:44, the figure — the church and the believer as the dwelling of God, called to holiness by his presence, 'ye are the temple of the living God... wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate,' 2 Corinthians 6:16-17, the truth that God's indwelling presence is the ground and motive of holiness, the body a temple of the Holy Ghost, 'know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost?' 1 Corinthians 6:19, the figure of the holiness that God's presence demands, the warning that uncleanness drives him away, the truth that to keep God's presence the people must be holy, his holiness the pattern and his presence the motive, the figure of the holiness required by the indwelling presence of God); God walks in the camp to deliver and give victory, his presence the ground of all (the indwelling God, Leviticus 11:44) — the presence (God in the camp); 'therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee' (the camp must be holy because of God's presence, no uncleanness lest he turn away, the holiness his presence demands, 1 Peter 1:16; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17; 1 Corinthians 6:19) — the figure (the church the dwelling of God, called to holiness, the providence, the holiness, the escaped slave to follow, vv.15-16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why must the camp be holy?

sides.position: Because the LORD God walks in the midst of it - the presence of God among his people demands their holiness, nothing unclean tolerated where he dwells lest he turn away (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16), a figure of the church and the believer as the dwelling of God, the temple of the Holy Ghost, called to holiness and separation by his indwelling presence (2 Corinthians 6:16-17; 1 Corinthians 6:19)

themes: For the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp, to deliver thee, and to give up thine enemies before thee; therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee

the presence of God among his people demands their holiness, nothing unclean tolerated where he dwells, the camp kept pure lest he turn away
a figure of the church and the believer as the dwelling of God, called to holiness by his indwelling presence (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16; 1 Corinthians 6:19)

schools.summary: The law that an escaped slave was not to be returned to his master: 'thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee' — the refuge — 'thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee': a slave who fled to Israel, especially from a foreign or cruel master, was not to be handed back (the witnesses noting the significance — the mercy of the law toward the fugitive and oppressed, the escaped servant, likely fleeing a heathen or harsh master, given refuge, not returned to bondage, the lesson — that God's law provides asylum for the oppressed, the fugitive not to be betrayed, the slave who sought shelter to be received, not delivered up, the humanity of the law that shelters the weak against the powerful, the truth that God is the refuge of the oppressed, his law sheltering those who flee cruelty, 'the LORD also will be a refuge for the oppressed,' Psalm 9:9, the figure — the asylum that the gospel offers to all who flee to Christ, the refuge for the bond and the oppressed, 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to preach deliverance to the captives... to set at liberty them that are bruised,' Luke 4:18, the truth that God's heart is toward the fugitive and the oppressed, the law a shadow of his mercy, the figure of the refuge given to the one who flees from bondage, the door not shut against him, the contrast with the cruelty that would return the fleeing slave, the mercy that receives him, the figure of mercy to the fugitive and refuge for the oppressed); the escaped servant not to be handed back to his master, the fugitive sheltered (the asylum, Psalm 9:9) — the law (not delivered up); 'the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee' (the slave who fled to Israel given refuge, the mercy to the oppressed who sought shelter, the refuge for the fugitive, Luke 4:18) — the figure (the asylum the gospel offers, Christ the refuge, the providence, the law, the fugitive to dwell freely among them to follow, vv.16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the law not to return the escaped slave establish?

sides.position: Mercy to the fugitive and refuge for the oppressed - the slave who fled a cruel or heathen master sheltered and not betrayed, the humanity of the law that shelters the weak against the powerful (Psalm 9:9), a figure of the refuge the gospel offers to all who flee from bondage to Christ, who came to set at liberty the bruised (Luke 4:18)

themes: Thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee

schools.summary: The fugitive to dwell freely and not be oppressed: 'he shall dwell with thee, even among you, in that place which he shall choose in one of thy gates, where it liketh him best: thou shalt not oppress him' — the escaped slave was to dwell freely where he pleased and not be oppressed (the he shall dwell with thee, even among you, dwelling among them, the in that place which he shall choose in one of thy gates, where it liketh him best, where he pleased, the thou shalt not oppress him, no oppression, the figure, the freedom and protection of the fugitive, dwelling where he chose, not oppressed, the truth that the refuge was full, the fugitive given liberty and shelter, the figure of the welcome of the oppressed into God's people, the freedom granted, the kindness that not only receives but protects the fugitive, the charge against oppressing the sheltered one, vv.15); the fugitive free to dwell where he pleased (the liberty) — the freedom (where he chose); 'thou shalt not oppress him' (the fugitive not to be oppressed, the protection of the sheltered, the figure, the kindness that protects the fugitive, the providence, the protection, the prohibition of cult prostitution to follow, vv.17-18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The prohibition of cult prostitution in Israel: 'there shall be no whore of the daughters of Israel, nor a sodomite of the sons of Israel' — no cult prostitute, male or female, was to be among God's people (the there shall be no whore of the daughters of Israel, no female cult prostitute, the nor a sodomite of the sons of Israel, no male cult prostitute, the figure, the prohibition of the cult prostitution of the heathen, the purity of God's people, the truth that the abominations of heathen worship, including ritual prostitution, were forbidden in Israel, the figure of the holiness that separates God's people from the filth of idolatry, the cult prostitution that defiled the heathen temples, banned from Israel, the purity required of God's people, vv.18); no female cult prostitute among God's people (the purity) — the prohibition (no whore); 'nor a sodomite of the sons of Israel' (no male cult prostitute, the abomination banned, the figure, the holiness that separates from idolatry, the providence, the prohibition, the hire not to be brought to the house of God to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

the purity that separates God's people from the filth of idolatry

schools.summary: That the wages of vice were not to be brought to God's house: 'thou shalt not bring the hire of a whore, or the price of a dog, into the house of the LORD thy God for any vow: for even both these are abomination unto the LORD thy God' — the wages of prostitution were not to be offered to God (the thou shalt not bring the hire of a whore, or the price of a dog, into the house of the LORD thy God, the wages of vice, the for any vow, not even for a vow, the for even both these are abomination unto the LORD thy God, the abomination, the figure, the wages of sin not to be offered to God, the abomination of such gifts, the truth that God will not be honoured with the gains of vice, the offering of the unclean rejected, the figure of the holiness of God who rejects polluted gifts, the unclean offering an abomination, the wages of sin no acceptable offering, God's house not to be defiled, the purity required even in offerings, vv.17); the wages of vice not to be offered to God (the abomination) — the prohibition (not into God's house); 'for even both these are abomination unto the LORD thy God' (the wages of vice an abomination to God, the polluted gift rejected, the figure, God rejects polluted offerings, the providence, the prohibition, the law of usury to follow, vv.19-20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not bring the hire of a whore, or the price of a dog, into the house of the LORD thy God for any vow: for even both these are abomination unto the LORD thy God
that the wages of vice were not to be brought to God's house
the wages of prostitution not to be offered to God, the unclean gift an abomination
God's house not to be defiled with the gains of vice

schools.summary: The prohibition of lending on usury to a brother: 'thou shalt not lend upon usury to thy brother; usury of money, usury of victuals, usury of any thing that is lent upon usury' — Israel was not to charge interest on loans to a fellow Israelite (the thou shalt not lend upon usury to thy brother, no interest to a brother, the usury of money, usury of victuals, usury of any thing, no interest of any kind, the figure, the prohibition of usury to a brother, the charity that lends without gain, the truth that the brother in need was to be helped, not exploited by interest, the figure of the love that lends freely, 'lend, hoping for nothing again,' Luke 6:35, the care for the poor brother, the loan a kindness, not a profit, the brotherly charity that forbids usury, vv.20); no interest to be charged a brother (the charity, Luke 6:35) — the prohibition (no usury); 'usury of money, usury of victuals, usury of any thing' (no interest of any kind to a brother, the full prohibition, the figure, the love that lends freely, the providence, the prohibition, the lawful usury to a stranger to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not lend upon usury to thy brother; usury of money, usury of victuals, usury of any thing that is lent upon usury
the prohibition of lending on usury to a brother
Israel not to charge interest on loans to a brother, the charity that helps the needy without gain
the loan a kindness, not a profit (Luke 6:35)

schools.summary: That usury might be charged a stranger but not a brother, with blessing on obedience: 'unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury; but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to in the land whither thou goest to possess it' — interest might be charged to a foreigner, not to a brother, and obedience would bring blessing (the unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury, interest to a stranger allowed, the but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury, not to a brother, the that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to, the blessing on obedience, the figure, the distinction between the stranger and the brother, the charity owed to the brother, the truth that the foreigner, a trader, might be charged, but the needy brother helped freely, the figure of the blessing that follows charity and obedience, God blessing the open hand, the brotherly love that lends without gain, rewarded by God, the blessing on the obedient in the land, vv.19); interest allowed to a stranger, not a brother (the charity) — the distinction (stranger and brother); 'that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to' (the blessing on the charitable and obedient, the reward of obedience, the figure, God blessing the open hand, the providence, the blessing, the law of vows to follow, vv.21-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury; but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to in the land whither thou goest to possess it
that usury might be charged a stranger but not a brother, with blessing on obedience
interest allowed to a foreigner but not a brother, the brotherly love that lends without gain rewarded by God
the blessing on the obedient and charitable in the land

schools.summary: The charge to pay a vow without delay, for God will require it: 'when thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee' — the obligation — 'when thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee': a vow once made to God was to be paid without delay (the witnesses noting the significance — the sacredness and binding force of vows made to God, a vow is a solemn promise to God, not to be neglected or delayed, God will require it, the lesson — that what is promised to God must be performed, the delay or breaking of a vow a sin, the vow voluntary, but once made, binding, the slackness in paying it a sin God will require, the truth that God holds men to their promises, 'better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay,' Ecclesiastes 5:5, 'when thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it; for he hath no pleasure in fools,' Ecclesiastes 5:4, the figure — the seriousness of all promises and commitments made to God, the call to faithfulness and integrity before him, the truth that the worship of God requires truth and performance, not empty words, the figure of the integrity that keeps its word to God, the vow a debt to be paid, the warning against the rashness that vows and the unfaithfulness that fails to pay, the call to weigh well what is vowed and then to perform it, the figure of the sacredness of vows and the obligation of promises to God); the vow once made to be paid without delay, the obligation binding (the performance required, Ecclesiastes 5:4) — the charge (slack not to pay); 'for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee' (God will surely require the vow, the failure to pay a sin, the integrity owed to God, the binding promise, Ecclesiastes 5:5) — the figure (the integrity that keeps its word to God, the providence, the charge, the freedom not to vow to follow, vv.22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the charge not to slack in paying a vow establish?

sides.position: The sacredness and binding force of vows made to God - the vow voluntary but once made obligatory, to be paid without delay, the slackness or breaking of it a sin God will require (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5), a figure of the seriousness of all commitments to God and the integrity that keeps its word, the worship of God requiring truth and performance, not empty words

themes: When thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee
the charge to pay a vow without delay, for God will require it (the crux of the sacredness of vows and the obligation of promises to God)
the binding force of a solemn promise to God, the vow voluntary but once made obligatory, the slackness or breaking of it a sin God will require
a figure of the seriousness of all commitments to God and the integrity that keeps its word (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5)

schools.summary: That to forbear vowing is no sin: 'but if thou shalt forbear to vow, it shall be no sin in thee' — vowing was voluntary, and not to vow was no sin (the but if thou shalt forbear to vow, the forbearing, the it shall be no sin in thee, no sin, the figure, the freedom not to vow, the vow voluntary, the truth that the vow was not commanded, but once made was binding, the figure of the liberty in things not commanded, the vow a free act, the contrast with the sin of breaking a vow, the safety of not vowing rashly, the freedom that warns against rash vows, vv.21); the freedom to forbear vowing (the liberty) — the forbearing (no vow); 'it shall be no sin in thee' (no sin in not vowing, the vow voluntary, the figure, the liberty in things not commanded, the providence, the freedom, the obligation once the word is spoken to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But if thou shalt forbear to vow, it shall be no sin in thee
that to forbear vowing is no sin
vowing voluntary and not to vow no sin, the liberty in things not commanded
the safety of not vowing rashly, set against the sin of breaking a vow

schools.summary: That what is once vowed must be kept and performed: 'that which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform; even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed unto the LORD thy God, which thou hast promised with thy mouth' — once the vow was spoken, it was binding and to be performed (the that which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform, the spoken vow kept, the even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed, the freewill vow binding, the which thou hast promised with thy mouth, the promise of the mouth, the figure, the binding force of the spoken vow, the word once given to be kept, the truth that the freewill offering, once vowed, became an obligation, the figure of the integrity that performs what the lips have promised, the word to God a debt, the spoken promise binding, the vow to be paid, the faithfulness that keeps the word given to God, vv.21); the spoken vow to be kept and performed (the obligation) — the spoken vow (kept); 'according as thou hast vowed... which thou hast promised with thy mouth' (the freewill vow, once spoken, binding, the promise of the mouth, the figure, the integrity that performs the promise, the providence, the obligation, the law of eating in the neighbour's field to follow, vv.24-25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: That which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform; even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed unto the LORD thy God, which thou hast promised with thy mouth
that what is once vowed must be kept and performed
once the vow was spoken it was binding, the word once given to God to be kept
the integrity that performs what the lips have promised

schools.summary: The liberty to eat the neighbour's grapes but not carry any away: 'when thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel' — the passer-by might eat his fill of grapes but not carry any off (the when thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill, the eating allowed, the but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel, no carrying away, the figure, the liberty to satisfy hunger, but not to plunder, the balance of kindness and right, the truth that the law allowed the hungry to eat but not the greedy to take, the figure of the generosity that feeds the passer-by, yet guards the owner's property, the kindness that relieves want without licensing theft, the charity and restraint together, vv.25); the liberty to eat grapes one's fill (the kindness) — the eating (one's fill); 'thou shalt not put any in thy vessel' (no carrying away, the owner's right guarded, the figure, kindness without licensing theft, the providence, the liberty, the same for the corn to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel
the liberty to eat the neighbour's grapes but not carry any away
the passer-by free to satisfy hunger but not to plunder, the generosity that feeds the hungry yet guards the owner's property
the balance of kindness and right

schools.summary: The liberty to pluck the neighbour's corn by hand but not reap it: 'when thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn' — the passer-by might pluck ears of corn by hand but not use a sickle to reap (the when thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand, the plucking allowed, the but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn, no reaping, the figure, the liberty to satisfy hunger by hand, but not to harvest, the balance of kindness and right, the truth that the law allowed the hungry to pluck but not to plunder with a sickle, the figure of the generosity that feeds the passer-by, the kindness the disciples used, 'his disciples were an hungred, and began to pluck the ears of corn,' Matthew 12:1, the charity that relieves want without licensing theft, the kindness and restraint together, vv.24); the liberty to pluck ears by hand (the kindness, Matthew 12:1) — the plucking (by hand); 'thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn' (no reaping with a sickle, the owner's right guarded, the figure, kindness without licensing theft, the providence, the liberty, the law of divorce to follow, ch.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn
the liberty to pluck the neighbour's corn by hand but not reap it
the passer-by free to pluck ears by hand to satisfy hunger but not to reap, the kindness the disciples used
the generosity that feeds the hungry yet guards the owner's property (Matthew 12:1)
